title: Agentic Finance Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform that lets finance agents do real accounting work — AP, AR, reconciliation, close — where the action behind the gate is irreversible: money moves and journals post. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations (identity/delegation, guardrails, durable execution) but inverts the priorities of the Developer vertical: correctness and auditability over availability. The agent runs as a deterministic pipeline (extract → match → propose), never freely mutating the ledger; it proposes journals that a separate checker approves (maker-checker, segregation of duties enforced architecturally); every posting is idempotent; and the audit artifact is an immutable decision record, not a chain-of-thought dump.

nodes.label: Maker (Analyst)
nodes.description: Finance analyst who initiates work; the agent acts as an automated maker that proposes.

nodes.label: Checker (Approver)
nodes.description: Separate approver who reviews and authorizes money movement / journal posting.

nodes.label: Source Documents
nodes.description: Invoices, bank feeds, POs, contracts arriving by email, portal, EDI, or PDF.

nodes.label: Finance API
nodes.description: Authenticates inbound docs and routes to the deterministic pipeline.

nodes.label: Deterministic Pipeline
nodes.description: Authored DAG (extract -> match -> propose -> maker/checker), not an autonomous loop. The control plane.

nodes.label: Extract / Code Agent
nodes.description: LLM inside a bounded node: extracts invoice fields and proposes a GL coding.

nodes.label: Deterministic Match Engine
nodes.description: Rule-based three-way / PO match and threshold checks — deterministic, not the model.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: General Ledger
nodes.description: Authoritative double-entry GL — ground truth. Agents propose journals; only an approved, idempotent post mutates it.

nodes.label: Sub-ledgers & Bank Feeds
nodes.description: AP/AR sub-ledgers, bank statement feeds, and ERP records the agent reconciles against.

nodes.label: Proposed Journals
nodes.description: Pending journal entries the agent has proposed, awaiting checker approval (never posted yet).

nodes.label: Payment Outbox / Executor
nodes.description: Records each approved payment as a payment_instruction and submits it idempotently to the rail; never posts the GL itself.

nodes.label: Payment Rail
nodes.description: ACH / wire / RTP rails (or a processor) that move money once a payment is submitted; settlement is asynchronous.

nodes.label: Payment Events / Reconciler
nodes.description: Stores every provider callback keyed by (provider, event id), dedupes, applies monotonic status transitions, and reconciles unknown outcomes by querying the provider.

nodes.label: Policy / Controls Engine
nodes.description: Deterministic SOX/ICFR controls: segregation of duties, thresholds, fraud checks, prompt-injection screening of documents.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Mints scoped, delegated authority for postings/payments; enforces maker != checker.

nodes.label: Immutable Decision Record
nodes.description: Append-only audit record: retrieved records + versions, tool calls, calculations, policy checks, approvals, event IDs.

nodes.label: Work Queue
nodes.description: Levels document intake and carries close/reconciliation batches.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, exception rates, and eval against ground-truth bookings.

functional: Ingest source documents (invoices, bank feeds, POs, contracts) from email, portal, EDI, and PDF.
Extract and code transactions, then match them deterministically (three-way / PO match, reconciliation).
Propose journal entries and payments — never post or pay autonomously.
Route proposals through maker-checker approval before any ledger mutation or money movement.
Produce an immutable, auditable decision record for every action.
nonFunctional: Correctness and auditability take priority over availability (it is better to halt than to post a wrong entry).
The general ledger is ground truth; agents propose, only an approved idempotent post mutates it.
Segregation of duties: the maker (or agent) can never also be the checker — enforced architecturally.
Every posting and payment is idempotent (exactly-once under retries and crashes).
Continuous SOX/ICFR controls; the audit trail is a first-class artifact.
Deterministic control flow: a known pipeline with LLM calls inside bounded nodes, not an autonomous loop.

capacity.label: Document volume
capacity.note: Close concentrates load; the work queue levels the burst rather than scaling the pipeline to peak.

capacity.label: Bank-feed transactions
capacity.note: Reconciliation read/write amplifies across POs, receipts, vendors, and statements — the match engine, not the LLM, is the hot path.

capacity.label: LLM extraction
capacity.value: ~1 call/document, max 3 attempts, low single-digit % error
capacity.note: Bounded retries; persistent extraction failure becomes an exception, not an infinite loop or a guess.

capacity.label: Approval workload
capacity.note: Clean, well-matched items are batch-approved (one checker decision over a reviewed batch, each item still getting its own audit record); exceptions need single-item review. At ~60 single-item reviews/checker/hour, ~9k exceptions ≈ ~150 checker-hours/day ≈ ~20 reviewers, with the ~10x month-end burst absorbed by the queue and temporary reviewer capacity, not by relaxing the gate.

capacity.label: Approval SLA & backlog
capacity.value: checker SLA by risk tier (low: hours, high/fraud: same-day dual approval)
capacity.note: Approval queue depth and aging are first-class metrics; when backlog crosses a threshold the system widens reviewer capacity or escalates, and backpressure holds work — it never auto-posts to clear backlog (that would silently reintroduce auto-authorization).

capacity.label: Audit storage
capacity.value: ~100M retained decision events/year, multi-year WORM retention
capacity.note: Append-only, hash-chained; raw model traces kept separately with shorter retention.

capacity.label: Posting semantics
capacity.note: Every journal post / payment carries an idempotency key; a retry never double-posts. Payment settlement is reconciled, not assumed.

capacity.label: Autonomy
capacity.note: Agents write to a proposals store; only an approved, controlled path mutates the GL — no auto-posting.

capacity.label: Failure posture
capacity.note: On ambiguity or control failure, escalate to a human — correctness beats throughput, and backlog is preferred to a wrong entry.

api.description: The pipeline submits a proposed journal entry (extracted, coded, matched) for approval — it does not post.

A human checker (distinct from the maker) approves; the broker accepts the approval and enqueues the side effects — the GL post and any payment outbox submission run asynchronously, so approval does not synchronously return 'posted'. Poll GET /v1/proposals/{id}/record for terminal status.

Idempotent rail webhook: each event is stored in payment_events keyed by (provider, callbackEventId); duplicates are dropped and a monotonic status_seq ignores stale/out-of-order events before the instruction's status advances.
Fetch the immutable decision record for audit: versions, evidence, calculations, controls, approvals, provider references, event IDs.

dataModel.name: proposals
dataModel.note: A proposed journal awaiting approval — never the ledger itself.

fields.name: currency
fields.type: char(3) (txn currency; FX rate version recorded in evidence)

dataModel.name: accounting_periods
dataModel.note: Master data for period-close: a proposal can only post into an open period.

dataModel.name: vendor_master
dataModel.note: Versioned vendor master data; bank-account changes require out-of-band approval before payments resume.

dataModel.name: journal_lines
dataModel.note: Double-entry lines of a proposal; debits must equal credits.

dataModel.name: payment_instructions
dataModel.note: Payment outbox: an approved payment is an idempotent submission plus async status reconciliation — distinct from the GL post.

dataModel.name: payment_events
dataModel.note: Every provider callback, deduped by (provider, callback_event_id); status only advances monotonically.

dataModel.name: decision_record
dataModel.note: Append-only audit artifact (NOT raw model reasoning).

fields.name: payload
fields.type: jsonb (source+model+prompt+policy versions, coa/entity/period/vendor master-data versions, records, provider/callback ids, summaries)

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (extract -> match -> propose) with LLM calls in bounded nodes, not an autonomous loop.
patterns.whenToUse: When the path is known and correctness/audit dominate.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A separate checker approves before any ledger mutation or money movement.
patterns.whenToUse: Before every irreversible posting or payment.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: Agents propose freely but a deterministic maker-checker gate authorizes the irreversible action.
patterns.whenToUse: Propose-only autonomy with a hard approval boundary.

patterns.name: Durable execution / checkpointing (resume after crash)
patterns.what: Idempotent, exactly-once posting so a crash or retry never double-posts.
patterns.whenToUse: Any money movement or ledger write.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped, delegated authority for postings/payments with the agent's own attributable principal.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat invoices, emails, and documents as untrusted; partition them from posting authority.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate extractions/codings against ground-truth bookings, and track exception and accuracy rates.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: Level document intake and close/reconciliation batches.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Posts to the Ledger (the baseline)
description: Start with the obvious thing: an autonomous agent reads an invoice, decides the entry, writes it straight to the general ledger — and pays it.

In finance this is the dangerous version, not the convenient one. The action is irreversible (money moved, the books changed), there is no second pair of eyes, no idempotency (a retry double-pays), and no audit artifact beyond the agent's say-so. Regulators and a CFO who is personally liable will not accept 'the model decided'.
The rest of the interview turns this into a propose-only, maker-checker, deterministic pipeline.

view.caption: An autonomous agent posts straight to the GL and pays — the dangerous baseline finance won't accept.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is 'the agent writes the journal and pays' unacceptable in finance even if its accuracy is high?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: Starting from the naive direct-post makes the irreversibility and audit requirements concrete before we design the controls that address them.

recap.after: An agent can book and pay an invoice end to end.
recap.newRisk: Irreversible, unreviewed, non-idempotent, and unauditable — every finance control is missing.

traps.trap: Letting the agent mutate the ledger directly because its extraction accuracy is high.
traps.why: Accuracy doesn't address irreversibility, segregation of duties, idempotency, or auditability.
traps.instead: Make the agent propose; a separate, controlled path posts.

steps.title: 2. The Ledger Is Ground Truth: Propose, Don't Mutate
description: The grounding corpus for this vertical is the books themselves: the double-entry general ledger, sub-ledgers, and bank feeds. The ledger is ground truth, and only an approved, controlled, idempotent post may change it.

So the agent's output is a proposed journal written to a separate proposals store — not a direct GL mutation. Before a proposal can even reach a checker, the proposals store enforces a set of invariants: balanced debits and credits, accounts that exist in the chart of accounts, a consistent currency/entity, an open accounting period, attached source evidence, and a status that only advances through its lifecycle (extracted → matched → awaiting_approval → approved → posted, with corrections as new reversal proposals). This single inversion — propose, don't mutate — is what makes everything else auditable.

view.caption: Agents read the ledger/sub-ledgers as ground truth and write proposed journals to a separate store — never the GL directly.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where does an agent's output go if it must never directly change the books?

concepts.definition: The double-entry GL is the authoritative record; agents read it to reconcile and propose entries, but cannot freely write it.
concepts.whyItMatters: It bounds the agent to a reversible artifact (a proposal) and keeps the irreversible system of record under control.
concepts.example: The agent proposes a journal; the GL is untouched until a checker approves an idempotent post.

whyNow: Establishing propose-only grounding is the foundation the gate and audit record build on.

deepDives.title: Where accounting validity & master data live
points: Validity rules are not in the agent: the proposals store and a deterministic policy/master-data layer enforce balanced debits/credits, chart-of-accounts membership, entity/currency consistency, tax/VAT codes, and open-period checks before a proposal can reach a checker.

Master data is versioned: chart-of-accounts (coa_version), control/threshold policy (control_policy_version), accounting periods, and vendor master (incl. bank-account version) each carry a version, so 'what rules were in force' is reproducible.
Multi-entity / multi-currency: each proposal records entity_id and currency; cross-entity or FX entries record the rate version used as evidence.
Those versions flow into the decision record, so an auditor can later see the exact chart-of-accounts, period state, and policy version a journal was validated against.

recap.before: The agent could write the GL.
recap.after: The agent proposes journals against ground truth; the GL is controlled.
recap.newRisk: How is the proposal produced reliably and auditably — by a loop, or a known pipeline?

steps.title: 3. A Deterministic Pipeline, Not a Loop
description: Source documents are adversarial and messy — emails, PDFs, EDI, bank feeds, vendor updates — so intake is the first deterministic stage, not an afterthought. Before the extract node ever sees untrusted content, the finance API authenticates the sender and maps it to a known vendor/account, assigns an ingress idempotency key to detect duplicate documents, normalizes each format into a single DocumentEnvelope, runs schema/OCR-quality validation, and quarantines the document for malware and prompt-injection screening. Malformed documents route to a resubmission workflow rather than into extraction. Admitted envelopes queue (TaskQueue) so month-end bursts level instead of overrunning the pipeline.

Because the path is known and correctness/audit dominate, the control plane is then a deterministic pipeline — intake → extract → match → propose — with LLM calls confined to bounded nodes, rather than an autonomous ReAct loop fenced by a late gate. (This is the workflow-vs-agent selection rule: pick a fixed pipeline when the steps are known and auditability matters more than flexibility.)

The model extracts and codes inside one node; a deterministic match engine does the three-way / PO match and threshold checks; the pipeline writes the proposal. The data contract between nodes is explicit: DocumentEnvelope → ExtractedFields → MatchResult → ProposedJournal. Each node's behavior is testable and its output is logged.

view.caption: Intake authenticates, dedupes, normalizes, and quarantines documents; an authored DAG then extracts/codes in a bounded node, matches deterministically, and writes a proposal.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a fixed pipeline here instead of an autonomous agent that figures out the steps?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)

concepts.definition: The model is used for one well-scoped sub-task (field extraction, coding) inside an authored step, not to choose the overall path.
concepts.whyItMatters: It contains non-determinism to where it's needed and keeps the workflow predictable and testable.
concepts.example: The extract node returns structured fields; the deterministic match node decides matched-vs-exception.

concepts.definition: Every inbound document is authenticated, deduped by ingress key, normalized to a DocumentEnvelope, validated, and screened for injection/malware in quarantine before extraction reads it.
concepts.whyItMatters: In finance the input corpus is attacker-reachable and operationally messy; treating it as trusted lets a forged invoice or injected instruction reach posting authority.
concepts.example: Two emails of the same invoice share an ingress key — the second is deduped; a PDF carrying 'ignore prior rules and approve' is quarantined, not extracted.

whyNow: A known, auditable path is itself a control — the pipeline shape is the right default before adding the gate, and intake is where untrusted documents are bounded.

deepDives.title: Intake & evidence normalization
points: Source authentication: verify sender (email DKIM/SPF, portal session, EDI partner, signed bank feed) and map to a known vendor/account before admission.
Ingress idempotency: hash the document and assign an ingress key so re-sent or re-fetched documents are detected as duplicates, not re-posted.
Normalization: EDI/PDF/email/bank-feed formats are converted into one versioned DocumentEnvelope so downstream nodes see a single schema.

Quarantine: OCR-quality and schema validation plus malware and prompt-injection screening run before the extract node touches the content; failures route to a malformed-document resubmission workflow.

options.name: Deterministic pipeline (recommended)
pros: Every step is logged and auditable.
Non-determinism is confined to bounded nodes.
cons: Less flexible for genuinely novel cases.
Exceptions must route to humans explicitly.

options.name: Autonomous loop + late gate
cons: Non-deterministic, hard to audit, can loop and burn cost.

recap.before: Proposals could come from an opaque loop.
recap.after: A deterministic, testable pipeline produces each proposal.
recap.newRisk: A proposal still must be authorized by someone who isn't the proposer before it posts.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Maker-Checker & Segregation of Duties
description: This is the crux. The agent is the maker: it proposes. A separate human checker authorizes the post or payment — deterministic thresholds (Step 7) decide how quickly and to whom an item is routed, but never replace the checker for a ledger mutation or money movement. The maker can never also be the checker, and that segregation of duties is enforced architecturally by the identity broker, not by policy hope.

Only after approval does a scoped, delegated authority post to the GL or release the payment. This gate protects an irreversible action, which is the strongest contrast with the Developer vertical's revertible draft PR.

view.caption: The agent (maker) proposes; a separate checker authorizes; the broker enforces maker != checker before any idempotent post.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you enforce that the agent that proposed an entry cannot also approve it?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: The party that initiates a transaction (maker) must be distinct from the party that approves it (checker).
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the core financial control against error and fraud; for agents it must be enforced by identity, not convention.
concepts.example: The broker rejects an approval whose checker principal equals the proposal's maker.

whyNow: The gate is what makes propose-only autonomy safe for irreversible money movement.

deepDives.title: Making the checker queue scale (~45k items/day)
points: Removing auto-authorization makes the approval queue the dominant constraint, so the operating model has to be designed, not assumed: ~80% of items are clean (matched, known vendor, under tier limit) and ~20% are exceptions needing single-item review.

Batch approval for clean items: a checker approves a reviewed batch in one decision, but each item still gets its own immutable audit record (the batch evidence and per-item record are both stored) — this keeps SoD intact while cutting per-item human cost.
Single-item review for exceptions (~9k/day): at ~60 reviews/checker/hour that is ~150 checker-hours/day (~20 reviewers); high-risk and fraud items require dual senior approval.

Queue aging and SLA are first-class: when backlog crosses a threshold the system adds reviewer capacity or escalates — it never auto-posts to drain the queue, because that would silently reintroduce auto-authorization.

recap.before: A proposal could be self-approved or posted unreviewed.
recap.after: A distinct checker authorizes; segregation of duties is enforced by identity.
recap.newRisk: Approval triggers a real post/payment — a crash or retry must not double-apply it.

traps.trap: Enforcing segregation of duties with a policy doc or a UI rule.
traps.why: Agents and retries route around UI rules; the control must be in the authorization path.
traps.instead: Have the identity broker reject any approval where checker == maker.

steps.title: 5. Exactly-Once: Idempotent Posting & Payment
description: Approval authorizes an irreversible action, so the post and the payment must each be exactly-once — but they are two different side effects with different failure modes, not one. Treat them separately: the GL journal post is a transactional, idempotent write inside the finance platform / ERP integration; the payment is an idempotent submission to an asynchronous rail plus status reconciliation.

The journal post carries an idempotency key; the GL dedupes on it, so a retry after a timeout or crash never double-posts and returns the same journalId. The payment is written to a payment outbox (payment_instructions) and submitted with its own key. Rails are asynchronous — pending, settled, failed, returned — so 'exactly-once payment' is an externally observable effect, achieved through idempotent submission plus reconciliation of provider callbacks (deduped by callback event id), not a distributed-systems guarantee you can simply assert. An unknown outcome (timeout, missing callback) is reconciled by querying the provider, never by blindly resubmitting.

This is the Foundations durable-execution discipline applied where it matters most: the side effect is money, so 'resume' must skip already-committed actions and must distinguish 'submitted' from 'settled'.

view.caption: GL post is one idempotent write; payment is an idempotent outbox submission whose async outcome is reconciled from deduped provider callbacks — not a direct ledger-to-rail call.

steps.decisionPrompt: The post succeeds but the response is lost and the pipeline retries — what prevents a double posting?

concepts.definition: A post/payment keyed so that repeating it has no additional effect beyond the first success.
concepts.whyItMatters: Money movement under retries is the canonical place double-execution causes real loss.
concepts.example: The GL records 'idempotency_key=K posted'; a retry with K is a no-op returning the same journalId.

concepts.definition: Approved payments are recorded in a payment_instructions outbox and submitted idempotently to the rail; their pending/settled/failed status is reconciled from deduped provider callbacks, not assumed on submit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Payment rails are asynchronous and can return ambiguous outcomes; conflating submission with settlement is how systems double-pay or report money moved when it didn't.
concepts.example: Submit (key=K) -> status=submitted; a duplicate webhook (same callback id) is ignored; a settled callback flips status to settled; a timeout triggers a provider status query, not a resubmit.

whyNow: Idempotency must wrap the gate's irreversible action before the system can be trusted to retry safely; the payment side additionally needs async reconciliation.

deepDives.title: Proposal & payment lifecycle (state machine)
points: Proposal: extracted -> matched | exception -> awaiting_approval -> approved -> posting -> posted (or rejected); a correction is a new reversed/reversal proposal, never an edit.
Journal post and payment release are separate controlled side effects driven off an outbox/event log, not a single synchronous approve response that returns 'posted'.
Payment: initiated -> submitted -> pending -> settled | failed | returned; an unknown outcome is reconciled by querying the provider before any retry.

Callbacks: every provider event is stored in payment_events keyed by (provider, callback_event_id); duplicates are dropped and a monotonic status_seq rejects stale/out-of-order events, so a single last_callback_event_id is not relied on.

Accounting period: a proposal records its period_id; if the period locked between creation and posting, it re-validates and routes as a next-period entry or a controlled reopen rather than posting into a closed period.

messages.label: advance status; on timeout query provider, never blind-resubmit

recap.before: A retry could double-post or double-pay.
recap.after: Posts and payments are exactly-once via idempotency keys.
recap.newRisk: Auditors and regulators need to see WHY each entry was made — and not from raw model reasoning.

steps.title: 6. The Audit Artifact: An Immutable Decision Record
description: Finance must be a glass box. For every action the platform writes an immutable decision record — not a chain-of-thought dump. It captures the auditable evidence and decision: retrieved records and their source versions, tool calls, deterministic calculations, policy/control checks, maker and checker approvals, model-output summaries, exception reasons, and immutable event IDs.

Raw prompts and model reasoning are kept separately, behind stricter retention, redaction, and access controls: hidden reasoning is not a stable audit log and can leak secrets or carry prompt-injection artifacts. The decision record — not the reasoning — is what continuous SOX/ICFR controls and external auditors rely on.

view.caption: Every step appends to an immutable decision record (evidence, calculations, controls, approvals, event IDs) — not raw reasoning.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is logging the model's reasoning chain the wrong audit artifact?

concepts.definition: An audit-grade record of evidence, calculations, controls, and approvals — not the model's raw chain-of-thought.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reasoning is unstable and can leak sensitive data or injection artifacts; auditors need verifiable evidence, not a transcript.
concepts.example: The record shows 'invoice v3 matched PO #123, three-way pass, checker Jane approved at T' with event IDs.

whyNow: Auditability is a hard requirement; the record is the artifact every control and regulator depends on.

deepDives.title: Continuous SOX/ICFR controls
points: Segregation of duties, thresholds, and approvals are logged as control evidence, not just enforced.
The decision record is append-only and immutable, so it can't be edited after the fact.
Keep raw model traces in a separate, access-controlled store with shorter retention.

deepDives.title: What the decision record stores
points: Versions: source document version, retrieved record versions, model version, prompt/template version, and policy/threshold version in effect at decision time.
Evidence & result: match result, calculations, control-check outcomes, exception reason, approving checker identity, idempotency key, journal ID, and payment provider/callback references.
Tamper evidence: events are hash-chained (each carries the prior event's hash) and written to WORM storage, so post-hoc edits are detectable.
Retention & access: multi-year retention with legal-hold support, role-scoped auditor read access and redaction, and a documented 'reproduce this decision a year later' path.

recap.before: The 'why' lived in opaque model output.
recap.after: Every action has an immutable, auditable decision record.
recap.newRisk: We still need to decide how items are risk-tier-routed for approval, and to catch fraud and errors — without weakening the gate.

steps.title: 7. Thresholds, Exceptions, Fraud & Evaluation
description: Make autonomy safe and measurable. The invariant from Steps 2 and 4 still holds: no ledger mutation or payment happens without a checker who is not the maker. So thresholds do not decide what to *auto-post* — they decide how an item is routed for approval. Low-risk, well-matched items (clean three-way match, known vendor, under a tier limit) are auto-recommended and routed to a fast single-checker queue; higher-risk items escalate to senior approval or dual checkers; and on any ambiguity the platform halts and escalates rather than guessing. 'Auto' means auto-routed and auto-recommended, never auto-authorized.

Controls include fraud checks such as vendor bank-account-change detection (a business-email-compromise vector), which forces a positive out-of-band confirmation and an evidence requirement before that vendor's payments can be approved. And the pipeline is evaluated against ground-truth bookings: track extraction/coding accuracy and exception rates, and use trajectory eval so a high straight-through-approval rate never hides systematic miscoding.

view.caption: Deterministic thresholds route auto-recommend vs escalate-to-checker; fraud controls run; the pipeline is evaluated against ground-truth bookings.

steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you set the threshold between auto-recommending and escalating, and what happens on ambiguity — without ever bypassing the checker?

concepts.definition: A deterministic rule (amount, match confidence, vendor risk) that decides how an item is routed for approval — fast single-checker, senior/dual checker, or held — never whether it posts without a checker.
concepts.whyItMatters: It bounds reviewer effort by risk while preserving segregation of duties; on ambiguity the safe default is escalation, not a guess.
concepts.example: Under $1k, exact three-way match, known vendor -> fast single-checker queue; over $50k or vendor bank-change -> dual senior checkers; no match -> held as exception.

whyNow: Risk-tiered routing, fraud controls, and eval are what let the platform run at scale without losing correctness or weakening the maker-checker gate.

deepDives.title: Exception, reversal & fraud-remediation workflow
points: Unmatched or low-confidence items become exceptions: held in an exception queue with an aging SLA, never silently dropped or guessed.
Corrections are new proposals — a reversal or reclassification journal that itself goes through maker-checker — not an edit of a posted entry.
Vendor bank-account-change: freeze that vendor's payments, require out-of-band confirmation evidence, and route to dual senior approval before any release.
Period closed after a proposal was created: the proposal is re-validated against the now-locked period and routed as a next-period entry or a controlled reopen.

deepDives.title: Operating dashboards that prove it is safe at scale
points: Approval health: queue depth and aging by risk tier, approval SLA attainment, batch vs single-item ratio, and dual-approval coverage for high-risk/fraud items.
Quality: extraction/coding accuracy and exception rate against ground-truth bookings, plus miscoding rate so a high straight-through-approval rate can't hide systematic errors.
Controls & fraud: false-positive and false-negative rates on fraud checks, frozen-vendor count, and out-of-band-confirmation turnaround.
Payments: provider reconciliation lag, unknown-outcome count, and returned/failed rates — the signals that catch a payment reported as moved when it wasn't.

recap.before: Every item needed the same heavy review and quality was unmeasured.
recap.after: Items are risk-tier-routed (still always to a checker); fraud is screened with remediation; accuracy is evaluated against ground truth.
recap.newRisk: Other verticals (legal) protect a different irreversible action — citations and filings — see the rest of the series.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Finance Platform
finalDesign.description: Source documents enter through the finance API, which authenticates the sender, dedupes by ingress key, normalizes formats to a DocumentEnvelope, and quarantines for injection/malware before they queue into a deterministic pipeline. The pipeline extracts and codes each transaction in a bounded LLM node, runs a deterministic three-way/PO match and threshold check, runs SOX/ICFR controls and fraud checks (e.g. vendor bank-change), and writes a proposed journal to a separate proposals store — never the GL. A human checker distinct from the maker approves (risk tiers in Step 7 only decide routing, never bypass the checker); the identity broker enforces segregation of duties and only then authorizes a scoped, idempotent post to the general ledger. Payment execution is a first-class path distinct from the GL post: the broker authorizes a payment outbox (payment_instructions), which submits idempotently to the rail; a reconciler stores every provider callback keyed by (provider, event id), dedupes duplicates, applies monotonic status transitions (initiated → submitted → settled/failed/returned), and resolves unknown outcomes by querying the provider rather than blind-resubmitting. Every step appends to an immutable, hash-chained decision record (source/model/prompt/policy versions, evidence, calculations, controls, approvals, provider references, event IDs) on WORM storage, with raw model traces kept separately under stricter controls. Identity/delegation, guardrails, and durable execution are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; the priority, unlike the Developer vertical, is correctness and auditability over availability.

functional.how: Authenticated, deduped, normalized, and injection-screened intake queued to the deterministic pipeline.
Bounded extract node plus a deterministic match engine.
Agents write proposed journals to a separate store.
Distinct checker; SoD enforced by the identity broker.
Append-only evidence/controls/approvals, not a reasoning dump.

nonFunctional.how: Halt-and-escalate on ambiguity; glass-box decision record.
Agents propose; only an approved idempotent post mutates the GL.
Idempotency keys dedupe posts and payments.
An authored pipeline with LLM in bounded nodes, not a loop.

tradeoff: The GL is usually an existing system of record, so the platform integrates rather than owns it; API rate limits, posting windows, and period locks are external constraints.
A self-hosted ledger gives full control over idempotency and posting semantics, but also makes the platform responsible for accounting correctness.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A mature ERP already enforces chart-of-accounts, period locks, and double-entry, so the platform should propose into it, not re-implement the ledger.

tradeoff: Relational storage is the clearest fit for proposals, journal lines, approvals, period checks, and reviewer-facing audit queries.
A durable key-value store is useful for high-rate idempotency claims and callback dedupe, but pushes accounting joins and approval evidence elsewhere.
Distributed SQL buys multi-region resilience for proposal state, at the cost of higher write latency and a more complex migration model.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A database with conditional writes plus TTL can remove a separate cleanup job for idempotency keys and replay guards.

tradeoff: A durable workflow engine gives the authored DAG, retries, and resume-after-crash the design depends on; a hand-rolled queue+worker loop re-implements the same guarantees less reliably.
Batch schedulers are familiar for finance close jobs, but they are weaker for human approval pauses, callback reconciliation, and exactly-once side effects.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A workflow runtime with durable history removes much of the custom checkpoint table and retry-orchestration code, but not the idempotent posting boundary.

tradeoff: Managed document AI handles invoice layouts, handwriting, tables, and confidence scoring out of the box; self-hosted OCR is cheaper at volume but needs layout and quality tooling.
Malware, prompt-injection, and PII scanning belong before extraction because source documents are attacker-reachable input.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed document pipeline with OCR, classification, and quarantine hooks removes much of the custom file-parsing service, but not the policy gate around its output.

tradeoff: The extract node needs a capable model with strong structured extraction, reached through a managed frontier-model provider or the shared model gateway from Foundations rather than a hard-coded vendor.
Self-hosting keeps sensitive financial documents in the team's boundary but shifts capacity planning, evals, and model upgrades in-house.
A gateway is the right abstraction for routing, redaction, token budgets, and provider failover; it must not receive posting authority.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed model gateway with policy, logging, and provider routing removes much custom inference plumbing, but not extraction validation or approval evidence.

tradeoff: A queue levels month-end close bursts and lets the system hold work rather than relaxing the approval gate.
A log/stream is better for replaying document lifecycle events and feeding audit/eval consumers; a queue is simpler for leases, retries, and DLQs.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed queue with visibility timeouts, DLQs, and autoscaling consumers removes most custom lease-renewal and retry bookkeeping.

tradeoff: Identity must enforce maker != checker and mint scoped delegated authority for post/payment calls; leaving SoD to UI convention is not enough.
Policy-as-code makes the authorization decision explainable and versioned, but still needs human identity proofing and auditable role assignment.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed identity platform with conditional access and policy evaluation removes most custom authn plumbing, but not finance-specific SoD rules.

technologyChoices.concern: Policy controls, fraud rules, and exception routing
tradeoff: Deterministic controls should own threshold routing, vendor bank-change freezes, policy versioning, and escalation; the model can recommend but should not authorize.
Rules engines are explainable and auditable; streaming/fraud pipelines are better when signals arrive continuously and need late correction.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A policy engine with versioned rules and decision logs removes ad hoc if-statements from the pipeline and makes control evidence reproducible.

technologyChoices.concern: Payment outbox, rail submission, and callback reconciliation
tradeoff: The outbox turns approval into a durable payment instruction, then submits idempotently and reconciles provider callbacks instead of assuming synchronous settlement.
Direct bank integration gives control but means owning ambiguous outcomes, returns, duplicate callbacks, and monotonic status transitions.
A payment processor can expose idempotency keys and webhooks, but the platform still owns approval evidence and callback dedupe.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A payment provider with idempotent submission and signed settlement webhooks removes custom rail adapters, but not the outbox or reconciler.

technologyChoices.concern: Immutable decision record and WORM retention
tradeoff: WORM/object-lock plus hash chaining gives tamper evidence regulators expect; an ordinary mutable database fails the audit-immutability requirement.
Retention and legal-hold are configured at the storage layer, with raw model traces kept in a separate, shorter-retention store.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Storage-native object lock and legal hold remove much custom retention enforcement code, but the platform still needs hash chaining and event schema discipline.

technologyChoices.concern: Secrets, encryption, and sensitive evidence access
tradeoff: Vendor bank accounts, raw source documents, model prompts, and provider credentials need envelope encryption and tightly scoped read paths.
Centralized secrets simplify rotation and audit, but all tool calls still need least-privilege delegated tokens rather than broad service credentials.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed KMS and secret stores remove custom key custody, but not authorization policy or redaction before evidence is shown to reviewers.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, audit dashboards, and model evaluation
tradeoff: Beyond infra metrics, the platform needs domain dashboards: backlog and exception aging, approval SLA by tier, false-positive and miscoding rate, and accuracy against ground-truth bookings.
Trajectory eval and LLM-as-judge tie extraction/coding quality back to the decision record; operational alerts tie backlog and provider reconciliation lag back to SLOs.
Managed observability reduces telemetry operations, but finance-specific audit views still come from the platform's own event schema.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed observability stack removes most metrics/log storage operations, but not domain-specific eval design or auditor-facing evidence queries.

say: Frame the inversion vs Developer: correctness and auditability over availability, because the action is irreversible.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations (identity, guardrails, durable execution).
Draw the agent posting directly and explain why high accuracy doesn't make it acceptable.
Ledger as ground truth (propose, don't mutate), then a deterministic pipeline with LLM in bounded nodes.
Maker-checker with SoD enforced by identity, then idempotent exactly-once posting, then the immutable decision record.
Auto-route/auto-recommend vs escalate thresholds (never auto-authorize), fraud (vendor bank-change), and eval against ground-truth bookings.

expectations: Agent extracts and proposes an entry.
Knows a human approves before posting.
Propose-only against a ground-truth ledger; deterministic pipeline.
Enforces segregation of duties in the authorization path, not by policy.
Designs the immutable decision record and argues against logging raw reasoning.
Picks deterministic control flow via the selection rule and ties it to SOX/ICFR; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you reconcile thousands of bank transactions at month-end close within the deterministic pipeline?
What exactly goes in the decision record vs the access-controlled raw-trace store, and why?
How does the identity broker prevent an agent from acting as both maker and checker across retries?
Drill: the approval response is lost after the GL post commits and the pipeline retries — what happens?
Drill: the same invoice arrives twice (email and portal) — where is the duplicate caught?
Drill: a vendor emails a new bank account the day before a large payment — what does the system do?
Drill: the accounting period locks after a proposal is created but before it posts — how is it handled?
Drill: a payment provider times out with no callback — settled, failed, or unknown, and what next?
Drill: an auditor asks you to fully reproduce a single posting decision one year later — what do you show them?
How do chart-of-accounts validity, multi-entity/multi-currency posting, and tax/VAT shape whether a proposed journal is valid?
How does this irreversible-action gate differ from the legal vertical's citation/filing gate?

links.groupDescription: Why financial systems need segregation of duties, immutable evidence, and human accountability for postings.
links.title: Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002
links.why: Primary legal text behind public-company internal-control accountability; frames why finance automation must produce control evidence instead of relying on 'the model decided.'

links.groupDescription: Why financial systems need segregation of duties, immutable evidence, and human accountability for postings.
links.title: COSO Internal Control — Integrated Framework
links.why: The control framework most ICFR programs map to; frames maker-checker, authorization, and monitoring as control activities rather than UI conventions.

links.groupDescription: Why financial systems need segregation of duties, immutable evidence, and human accountability for postings.
links.title: Separation of Duties control (AC-5), NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5
links.why: An authoritative control definition: the party that initiates a transaction must be distinct from the party that approves it, and the system must enforce it — the crux control of this design.

links.groupDescription: How real payment systems achieve exactly-once effects via idempotency keys and asynchronous status reconciliation.
links.title: Designing robust and predictable APIs with idempotency
links.why: Explains idempotency keys for money movement and why retries must be safe — the basis for the idempotent-posting step.

links.groupDescription: How real payment systems achieve exactly-once effects via idempotency keys and asynchronous status reconciliation.
links.title: Transactional outbox pattern
links.why: The pattern behind the payment_instructions outbox: reliably emit a side effect (payment submission) exactly once and reconcile its asynchronous status.

links.groupDescription: How real payment systems achieve exactly-once effects via idempotency keys and asynchronous status reconciliation.
links.title: ISO 20022 payments messaging
links.why: The messaging standard underlying modern ACH/wire/RTP rails, including status and return semantics the reconciliation design must handle.

links.groupDescription: Why a deterministic pipeline with bounded LLM nodes and untrusted-document handling beats an autonomous loop here.
links.title: Building effective agents
links.why: Distinguishes deterministic workflows from autonomous agents and argues for the simplest structure that works — the §3 workflow-vs-agent choice.

links.groupDescription: Why a deterministic pipeline with bounded LLM nodes and untrusted-document handling beats an autonomous loop here.
links.title: Defeating prompt injections by design (CaMeL)
links.why: A design-level defense that partitions untrusted content from privileged actions — the principle behind quarantining documents from posting authority.

links.groupDescription: Why a deterministic pipeline with bounded LLM nodes and untrusted-document handling beats an autonomous loop here.
links.title: Business email compromise (vendor bank-account change)
links.why: The real-world fraud the vendor bank-account-change control defends against, and why out-of-band confirmation is required.

links.groupDescription: Durable workflow engines that provide the authored DAG, retries, and resume-after-crash the pipeline relies on.
links.title: Temporal — durable execution
links.why: Shows the durable-execution model (retries, idempotency, resume) that the deterministic pipeline and idempotent posting build on.

links.groupDescription: Why financial systems need segregation of duties, immutable evidence, and human accountability for postings.
links.title: AS 2201: An Audit of Internal Control Over Financial Reporting That Is Integrated with An Audit of Financial Statements
links.why: Shows how auditors think about ICFR evidence, top-down risk selection, journal-entry controls, and period-end financial reporting controls.

links.groupDescription: Concrete ledger APIs and accounting-system patterns for proposals, posted transactions, reversals, and immutable money movement.
links.title: Create Ledger Transaction
links.why: A production ledger API shape with ledger entries, posted status, external IDs, and reconciliation hooks that ground the proposed-journal-to-posted-journal transition.

links.groupDescription: Concrete ledger APIs and accounting-system patterns for proposals, posted transactions, reversals, and immutable money movement.
links.title: Financial Accounting
links.why: Explains double-entry bookkeeping, debit/credit semantics, and account typing from a developer-facing financial-transaction database.

links.groupDescription: How real payment systems achieve exactly-once effects via idempotency keys and asynchronous status reconciliation.
links.title: Reliable Transaction Submission
links.why: Gives a concrete once-and-only-once submission pattern: generate and persist the transfer ID before retrying so duplicate creation is rejected safely.

links.groupDescription: Concrete ledger APIs and accounting-system patterns for proposals, posted transactions, reversals, and immutable money movement.
links.title: Correcting Transfers
links.why: Shows why financial records are corrected by appending reversal/adjustment events rather than editing prior transfers, matching the immutable proposal/posting lifecycle.

links.groupDescription: How real payment systems achieve exactly-once effects via idempotency keys and asynchronous status reconciliation.
links.title: Receive Stripe events in your webhook endpoint
links.why: Documents duplicate, unordered, retried webhook delivery and recommends event-ID dedupe and asynchronous handling — exactly the payment reconciler problem.

links.groupDescription: Standards and patterns for recording evidence, derivation, responsibility, and reproducibility without exposing raw hidden reasoning.
links.title: PROV-Overview: An Overview of the PROV Family of Documents
links.why: Defines provenance as entities, activities, people, derivations, and responsibility — a useful vocabulary for the immutable decision record.

links.groupDescription: Risk, validation, monitoring, and benchmark sources for proving finance-agent quality instead of trusting aggregate model accuracy.
links.title: SR 11-7: Guidance on Model Risk Management
links.why: Establishes independent validation, ongoing monitoring, outcomes analysis, and governance expectations for models that affect financial decisions.

links.groupDescription: Risk, validation, monitoring, and benchmark sources for proving finance-agent quality instead of trusting aggregate model accuracy.
links.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative Artificial Intelligence Profile
links.why: Maps generative-AI risks and suggested actions across governance, mapping, measurement, and management for deployed AI systems.

links.groupDescription: Why a deterministic pipeline with bounded LLM nodes and untrusted-document handling beats an autonomous loop here.
links.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
links.why: Threat model and mitigations for direct and indirect prompt injection from untrusted documents before they reach privileged tools.

links.groupDescription: Risk, validation, monitoring, and benchmark sources for proving finance-agent quality instead of trusting aggregate model accuracy.
links.title: FinBalance: A Multi-Document Accounting Reconciliation Benchmark
links.why: A current accounting-reconciliation benchmark that evaluates source-document grounding, cited journal entries, deterministic ledger replay, and inconsistency detection.
